title: Introducing FlatList
description: The performant way to render long lists of data.

slides.title: Why FlatList? 🤔
slides.content:
# The Problem with ScrollView

If you have a list with hundreds or thousands of items, `ScrollView` renders them all at once. That's memory‑intensive and can make your app sluggish.

**Enter FlatList** – it renders items **lazily**: only the ones currently visible on screen, plus a few offscreen. As you scroll, it reuses views and discards off‑screen items.

* `FlatList` is a **virtualized list**.
* It's the go‑to component for long, dynamic lists.

slides.title: FlatList Basics 📋
FlatList Example

quizData.question: What is the main advantage of FlatList over ScrollView for long lists?

options.text: It renders only visible items, saving memory

quizData.explanation: FlatList virtualizes its content, rendering only what's on screen, which improves performance for large lists.

slides.title: The `keyExtractor` Prop 🔑
slides.content:
## Providing Stable Keys

By default, FlatList uses the item's index as the key. This can cause issues if the list order changes (e.g., sorting).

It's best to provide a `keyExtractor` that returns a unique identifier for each item.

```jsx
<FlatList
  data={DATA}
  keyExtractor={(item) => item.id}
  renderItem={({ item }) => <Text>{item.title}</Text>}
/>
```

Now React can track each item even if the list is reordered.

slides.title: Important ⚠️
slides.content: Always provide a `keyExtractor` if your data has unique IDs. If you don't, and the list changes, you might see strange behaviour (e.g., incorrect text in items after reordering).
